Q1: I tried to organize my study routine around listening to lectures and recorded materials, since I learn best by hearing things explained. With 12 online courses, I had to spread my 19 hours across the week pretty carefully. I usually set aside blocks in the morning when my apartment is quiet, putting on headphones and going through video content. It wasn't always perfect, but I tried to stay consistent even when resources were tight.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on deadlines and what was due that week. I tried to keep some regularity, like studying most mornings, but when assignments piled up I'd shift everything around. Sometimes I had to prioritize courses with urgent deadlines over others, which meant my schedule got messy. Being a student with limited resources means I can't always control when I have access to a quiet space or stable internet.
Q3: Usually what prompted me to log in was checking if new lecture recordings were posted or if there were announcements about assignments. I also logged in when I felt scared about falling behind, which happened more often than I'd like to admit. Sometimes I'd just log in to reassure myself that I hadn't missed anything important. The fear of missing a deadline was a big motivator for me.
Q4: Yes, after a few weeks I got into a habit of checking the LMS every morning with my coffee. It became almost automatic, like checking messages. I think it happened because I was scared of missing something important, so logging in became a way to calm that anxiety. Once it was part of my morning routine, it felt less stressful to keep up with everything.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely the most useful for me because I'm an auditory learner. I'd listen to them multiple times, sometimes while doing other things around my small apartment. The discussion forums were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives through their written words helped concepts click for me. I spent over 186 minutes watching videos because that's genuinely how I learn best.
Q6: I often minimized my use of the reading materials and PDFs because reading isn't my strongest way of learning. I'd skim them but wouldn't deeply engage unless absolutely necessary. Some of the supplementary resources also felt redundant if the video already covered the topic well. With limited time and energy, I had to prioritize what actually helped me understand the material.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to what would be on exams or helped me understand a confusing concept. I also valued activities where I could hear explanations rather than just read them. If something felt like busywork or wasn't clearly connected to my grades, I was less motivated to invest time in it. My time is precious and I have to be strategic.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because I could approach them without that scared feeling in my stomach. Required activities came with pressure and fear of consequences, which sometimes made it harder to focus. With optional ones, I could engage more freely if the topic interested me, but I often skipped them when time was tight. The pressure of required work always took priority.
Q9: Yes, absolutely. I downloaded lecture recordings and listened to them on my phone when I didn't have reliable internet at home. I also made audio notes for myself, recording my own voice summarizing concepts so I could listen back later. Sometimes I'd study with a classmate and we'd talk through the material together, which was really effective for me but wouldn't show up anywhere in the system.
Q10: There were many times I was studying intensely but it wouldn't show in the LMS. Like when I'd replay downloaded lectures while commuting or doing chores, or when I'd have long phone conversations with classmates working through problems together. I also spent time just thinking through concepts while walking or lying in bed. That mental processing was real studying, but completely invisible.
Q11: Definitely. I would download video lectures once and then watch them multiple times offline over the following days. My internet isn't always reliable, so downloading was essential for me. I'd also screenshot important slides and review them on my phone throughout the week. The LMS would only show that initial download, not the hours of offline review that followed.
Q12: There were moments when fear really paralyzed me, especially when I fell behind on assignments and my completion rate dropped. When that scared feeling got too intense, I'd sometimes avoid logging in altogether because facing it felt overwhelming. Financial stress also affected me since I have limited resources, and worrying about money made it hard to concentrate on coursework. Those were the hardest weeks.
Q13: Workload from having 12 online courses definitely affected how I used the LMS. When I was overwhelmed, I'd focus only on the most urgent tasks and ignore everything else. Fatigue was real too, especially when I'd been listening to lectures for hours. Sometimes I'd log in but be too tired to actually engage meaningfully with the content, just going through the motions.
Q14: Yes, there were times when I'd have the LMS open and be scrolling through materials but my mind was elsewhere. When I was really scared about deadlines or stressed about money, I couldn't actually absorb what I was looking at. I'd watch videos but realize at the end that I hadn't really heard anything. Those moments felt frustrating because I was trying but not succeeding.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments even when I knew they were important, which is why my completion rate is only around 26%. Sometimes the fear of doing something wrong made me avoid starting at all. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow when I felt more confident, but then tomorrow would come with the same anxiety. It's something I'm working on through my CBT sessions, actually.
Q16: Deadlines were huge motivators for me, sometimes in unhealthy ways. I'd often wait until pressure built up enough to overcome my fear of starting. The days before deadlines, my LMS activity would spike as I frantically tried to catch up. Looking back, I wish I'd spread things out more evenly, but that scared feeling made it hard to engage until the deadline forced me to.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback, it gave me confidence to keep going and engage more with the course. But negative feedback sometimes made me scared to try again, and I'd avoid that course for a while. The CBT sessions have helped me understand these patterns better and not take feedback so personally.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot. Courses with clear, regular schedules were easier for me to follow because I could build them into my routine. When expectations were unclear or deadlines were scattered, my anxiety increased and I'd sometimes disengage. Having recorded lectures that I could listen to multiple times was really important for my learning style.
Q19: Having video content made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. The discussion forums were also helpful because they gave me another way to process information. What made it harder was when courses relied heavily on dense readings or had confusing navigation. Also, courses that didn't post materials in advance made it hard for me to plan around my limited resources.
Q20: I think sometimes students are trying really hard but struggling to complete work due to fear or confusion. They might be logging in repeatedly, watching videos, reading discussions, trying to understand, but then freezing when it comes to actually submitting something. That fear of judgment or failure can create a gap between effort and output. I've experienced that myself.
Q21: That makes sense to me because some students might be really efficient or learn well offline. They might download materials once and study them thoroughly without needing to log in repeatedly. Or they might have strong study groups or external resources. The LMS activity doesn't capture all the real learning happening outside the system, which can be substantial.
Q22: I think course logs can show patterns like when students are active, what resources they access, and general engagement trends. But they miss the quality of that engagement, whether someone is actually absorbing material or just clicking through. They also miss offline studying, emotional states, and the real reasons behind behavior. My scared feelings don't show up in any log.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and connect with the material, not just completing tasks. It's about thinking about course concepts even when I'm not logged in, discussing ideas with others, and caring about learning. For me, it also means pushing through fear to participate even when I'm scared. True engagement is internal and can't be fully measured by clicks.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I struggled with fear and how hard I worked to overcome it through CBT sessions. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures offline or the conversations with classmates that helped me understand difficult concepts. It also wouldn't show how proud I feel when I push through anxiety to participate, or how much my limited resources affect my study patterns.
